content.text:
The two flags serve different purposes in the classification pipeline:

**`selectionSummary.needsAi`** (line 2655) - Suggestion from Multi-Label Selection stage (S7)
- Set by stage 7 based on confidence thresholds in that stage
- Indicates the multi-label selector _thinks_ AI might help

**`needsAiClassification`** (line 2667) - Decision from AI Routing stage (S8a)  
- Set by stage 8a after evaluating cost/benefit of AI
- The _actual_ routing decision

Looking at lines 1198-1203, AI routing made this decision:

```json
"aiRouting": {
  "routingDecision": "skipAi",
  "highConfidenceThreshold": 1,
  "aiRequiredThreshold": 0.8,
  ...
  "reason": "Medium confidence...but below AI routing threshold"
}
```

The logic at `/workspaces/gmail-automation/packages/classification-engine/src/stages/ai-routing.ts:274-287`:

```typescript
// Conservative approach: medium confidence usually benefits from AI
if (primaryScore >= aiRequiredThreshold) {  // 0.28 >= 0.8 → FALSE
  return { routeToAi: true, ... };
} else {
  return { routeToAi: false, ... };  // ← This path taken
}
```

**Why skip AI at 0.28 confidence?**
- Confidence (0.28) is above low threshold (0.2) but below AI routing threshold (0.8)
- AI routing conservatively avoids API costs when confidence is too low to benefit
- The reasoning: financial classification at 0.28 is low-quality, but AI likely won't improve it enough to justify the cost

This is cost-optimization logic - AI routing considers whether the AI will provide enough value given the current confidence level.
